sentences.text: --your father.
And that His trips to Germany became less frequent.
Yes, they became less frequent.
Then legal import of money from our apartment stopped.
Germany forbade to export currency.
[INAUDIBLE] official.
I think it must've started in '35.
I distinctly remember that one Christmas the parcel that my father used to send to his manager of the apartment-- Otto Lehman, I remember-- every year.
He was his very close friend, he was a Communist-- and he had an apartment in this house--
--in Germany, in this house.
And every year my father sent him for Christmas a very beautiful, good parcel.
He used to go to Katowice, and there was a magnificent delicatessen shop.
And I accompanied him in winter on these trips.
And it was all set in Christmas, like forests, with reindeers and Santa Claus.
And Bedzin was a Jewish town.
You didn't see things like that.
And he sent him presents and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no, not rabbit.
The other one.
Hare and smoked and things like that.
Beautiful delicatessen.
And one January we got the parcel back that Germany does not gifts-- does not need gifts from you.
That was stamped on the parcel.
Stamped on the parcel, [GERMAN].
That would be '36, '37.
The apartment was in Berlin Pankow, which belonged after the war to East Germany.
The irony is, it wasn't bombed-- it wasn't even damaged.
And it's still there.
Oh, I made claims.
Sure.
I hope that I will be able to sell it.
But that's beside the point.
So my father didn't need a passport.
He had a passport that was ready for five years.
So he could decide-- there was a train from Katowice direct to Berlin.
So he could decide on Sunday that he goes to Berlin.
He rang up his friend.
And there was always a room ready for him.
And he was to go.
And the time of smuggling money started.
And the smuggling of money, for that, he hired people.
And I distinctly remember that they came at night, because they-- my parents did not want me to know about it.
And the more precautions they knew to keep a secret, the more adamant I was, what was the secret about?
And the secret was?
The secret was that he was smuggling the money for my father.
And for doing the smuggling--
He was smuggling the deutschmarks.
From Germany, you could smuggle only deutschmarks.
What my father did with the deutschmarks, I don't know.
I presume he either changed them to dollars or to zlotys.
And once, I caught them.
I entered the room, and they were squeezing out a toothpaste tube, and there was the money.
They squeezed out.
That's how it was smuggled.
Don't forget that 100 deutschmark was more than 100 deutschmark today.
And for that, my father paid him 30% of the money.
Maybe he had two toothpastes.
Maybe he did that every day.
I don't know.
I thought maybe he did it twice a week.
I don't even know whether he was Jewish, German, Polish, or whatever.
Not-- not at all.
The change-- not in the economic life, not in the cultural life, and not in the political ambiance of the times.
The change started when echos and clouds on the horizon-- started to appear in 1937 when the first refugee from Germany started to arrive to Poland.
I think it was '37 they started to arrive to a border town, which was called [?
Swinoujscie ?] and dumped on the border.
Poland was not prepared to take them and Germany didn't want them.
They were people who originally came from Poland.
Originally means before the first World War.
Mind you, there were only 20 years between the two wars, but could've been a third generation of the people whose parents never bothered to change passport.
The whole structure of society in Europe was rather static, not as mobile as it is now.
You didn't need to change your passport.
You didn't travel.
Very few people traveled abroad.
Then they would have gone five years later, three years later.
Oh, they would have been expelled together with the German Jews.
But they were the first [NON-ENGLISH], the avant garde.
And of course, we, like always in time of stress, in time of looming tragedies, you try to justify that.
And you said, it happened to Polish Jews in Germany.
But we are Polish Jews in Poland.
It cannot happen to us.
Well, nobody saw it, perceived, it as such.

As I told you, the justification was-- the explanation, justification, and interpretation of the whole act-- the press was full of the horrible conditions under which these people live, dumped on the border.
I think it was autumn.
And immediately, representative of Jewish communities from larger towns were dispatched there.
And a certain amount to of group of people were allotted to Jewish towns.
It was usually somebody who knew somebody who knew somebody who knew their grandfather, and bang, landed in Bedzin.
My mother was, for a-- I think, at least eight months-- involved in kitchen-- soup kitchens.
I remember that I hadn't seen her for days.
And so were other Jewish women, the ladies.
I couldn't tell you exactly what number.
Look, A-- I was not interested in statistics.
B-- it did not penetrate my world.
And if it did, it's only by the presence of one or two boys or girls of my age.
None of them stayed.
There is one boy that I remember went to Palestine with his family.
And that was the very first act.
However, few families-- very few families-- really didn't have anybody because their parents lived in Germany for so long that all links with the past was completely severed.
And this particular German couple-- she was a millinery of ladies hats.
I don't know what he was.
Decent, soft spoken, very cultured person.
And this family came to live with my aunt who lived above us.
And they ate in our place.
Very soon-- my aunt had a larger apartment because she occupied the whole second floor, while we shared the first floor together with my grandmother.
So she had so much larger apartment.
And they stayed till the outbreak of the war.
And she very soon started to work as a millinery.
And I really don't remember them after that.
She wasn't Jewish.
She wasn't Jewish.
And she accompanied her husband.
I don't remember their names, but I see their faces.
And I-- what else I know, they were allowed to take a suitcase.
And in that suitcase was one pair of silver candle-- candlesticks.
I don't.
However, there was another man, a single man.
And I remember him from the time also that he ate with us.
What my mother arranged-- she used to arrange a roster among her friends that those German refugee-- refugees-- eat one decent meal, at least, in different families.
But this couple that lived with my aunt, they ate every day with us.
So it was divided until they could stand on their feet, and she could earn a little money, and so on.
So they lived with my aunt and they ate with us.
But this single man, I remember him during the war, how he was getting shabbier, and shabbier, and shabbier.
No.
Oh, that was my mother and my father.
There was nothing--
There was no alternative.
That's how it was.
You have to help people.
That's how-- there was-- there was not a situation where you could say 'or.'
That's how it has to be.
Somebody's in need, you help him.
My brother--
My brother used to come back for every holiday.
Since--
Not term holiday.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
Yes, for three months.
Except on one holiday in 1939.
And there-- they were-- there was a two-fold reason for it.
By that time, my brother finished his study of veterinary science.
And in order to obtain French citizenship, or so-called nostrification, as a foreigner, he has to have an uninterrupted residence for two years.
And that coincided in 1939.
The other reason was because I, after my matriculation, was going to France.
My father applied for permission to send foreign currency to France for upkeep of two students.
And he started to send the money prior to my arrival, because I am a girl, and I had to be better looked after than the boy.
So my brother stayed in France, A-- for his nostrification, B-- in order that he can receive the money.
And when the war broke out, he was in that fortunate position that he had money that was destined actually for me.
I never made it to France for my studies.
So the war broke out, and he was in France.
The war broke out, and he was in France.
Well, he had to flee.
He eventually fled to-- I don't know whether that-- he was-- he was rounded up.
He was in the place called Les Essarts, which is in Vendee, which is a very agriculture part of France.
And I don't know whether it went under the-- under Vichy government or not.
But whatever the case was, he was rounded up, and he was actually taken out by [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Martinique.
A person from Martinique, a non-Jew, who was a veterinarian.
And he came into the camp, and he was looking for veterinarian surgeons.
And he took my brother out.
And he was responsible for him.
And then my brother fled.
And I don't know exactly where it was.
But it was-- or perhaps this Martinique person gave him a farm somewhere high in the mountains.
It could have been in Ardennes-- as far as that.
I'm not sure.
And they gave him a farm with a land that wasn't [INAUDIBLE] rebel for a number of years.
And he said, I don't want any money from you, but you look after the farm, and whatever you can get out of the soil is yours.
And the soil was impossible to yield a potato.
By that time, they had Claudett, who was three years old.
So I think it must've been '40, '41.
He was married to a non-Jewish person, which he-- he wasn't the first one in our family.
And-- and she's older than him.
And she was very supportive to him.

And because he had a non-Jewish wife, that was one more reason why he went into hiding-- not to create a situation where she would be confronted with a choice, or with an order to abandon her husband, and so on.
And they have a three-year-old daughter and for five-- for four years, the child did not know that her father is hiding in the attic.

Again, they did not want to expose her to a situation whether she might be accosted by a militia, by a French militia, by a raid of maquis who are looking constantly for food and for medications, if it would become known that there is a veterinary surgeon.
And he came down from the attic only at night.
And at the end of the war-- no, four years-- no, maybe it was three years or two years.
Whatever the length of time was, the child did not know that the father is with them.
And I think it was also psychologically a very good move, because she didn't have a double standard.
She was not told to hide something, to deny something, to negate something.
And that was it.
While we are still on subject of France, I want to tell you that out of my five uncles, all survived, all in hiding.
Except one.
No, in southern France.
Except one, who stayed in Paris.
And he was saved from the transport going from [NON-ENGLISH] by engine driver.
He taught-- he jumped the transport.
The engine driver gave him a sign to jump the transport.
He became level with the engine.
He opened the door to his cabin, dragged he in-- dragged him in-- and started to move the train down the railways, against the orders till the transport left.
And there was a price on his head.
No.
On my uncle's head.
Because there's German bureaucracy.
One was missing from the transport.
Yes, absolutely.
Absolutely.
It was his name and address in alphabetical order.
One is missing.
And then, at the end of his shift, he took him to his home.
He fed him, dressed him, or whatever it was.
And then they went into hiding.
Yes, both he and his wife.
But I am not quite sure whether they joined the family.
The family-- they couldn't join the family, because the family didn't exist as a unit anymore.
Everybody had different papers.
Some were-- none of them was-- were in hiding.
They all lived on forged papers.
They were all French.
But my uncle, the one who jumped the transport, had an accent, which was very good for the Germans.
They didn't recognize that.
But there were nasty piece of militias, of Frenchman militia around.
And from them they had to--
--to beware.
Now-- now the effect on us.
Til the year of about '40, '41, we were in touch with my brother.
Then-- there is a piece of geography that I have to add to the picture.
Early in the peace, we were integrated in the Great German Reich.
Or '39 or early '40.
Because Bedzin was in Upper Silesia.
Silesia had a lot of ethnic Germans.
We call them ethnic now.

At that time, they were called Volksdeutsche, which means they were Germans, of German origin, who, as a result of the First World War partition, division changes, and exchanges, found themselves on the territory that was allocated to Poland.
And very early, we were integrated in the German Reich.
And irony of irony-- of ironies, we started to receive, officially, income from our apartments.
That was legal, through the bank.
It was pittance.
I remember, once we received 50 mark.
But we were called to the bank.
We were allowed to leave the restricted area where we lived.
My father entered the post office, wearing an armband, as a Jew, with a piece of paper that he comes to collect 50 marks.
That is German.
The ultimate in German bureaucracy.
It started very early.
We were marked very early.
I cannot give you exactly the date, whether it was end of '39 or, I think-- or beginning of 1940.
At first, it was a white armband with a blue star of David.
We were not Poles.
We were never Poles.
We were Jews who lived in Poland.
What, integration?
The integration was ethnic and geographic.
It doesn't matter where the Jew lived-- he remained a Jew.
The reason-- the only other effect that this integration could have had of us, that in-- up till-- up till 1943, we were treated, perhaps, as the few remaining German Jews.
They didn't bother us very much.
Our ghetto was small.
It was a very productive ghetto.

Because we had a-- we had a German manager-- a German German-- who came to Bedzin and established-- who came to Bedzin as an employee of SS, and told them, I am surrounded by artisans Jews-- tailors, shoemakers, furriers, cobblers-- and why not to utilize them?
And he-- and they have got tools.
And they can bring their tools, and their upkeep is not great, and their skills are enormous.
And let them work.
I have got-- I didn't prepare that photograph.
And he-- he was a disabled person.
He was a polio victim.
He was also a hemophiliac.
And with great pride, and in great humility, I am telling you that a few weeks ago, I got a notification from the [NON-ENGLISH] from Israel that he was honored as one of the Righteous Among Nations.
Upon my instigation.
This was the German, Alfred Rossner upon my instigation.
Well, it's a whole long story.
We are jumping ahead a little bit, but not very much.
It's a-- he was a very unusual figure.
And he was-- he was fighting the system, and paid for it with his own life.
He was hanged by the Gestapo for helping and abetting Jews in January 1944.
Definitely.
He prolonged the life of the ghetto.
In any day-- he made it productive.
He offered the Germans a production of winter uniforms to be sent to the Russian front, which was enormous.
In the height of-- of the ghetto, he employed 6,000 Jews.
Yes, and each worker, thanks to him, was allowed to protect two members of his family-- either his or her parents, or a spouse and a child.
So in fact, there were 18,000 Jews untouched.
And he was in constant disagreement and in constant friction with the Judenrat, who sadly had to produce Jews for deportatiom And here there was a ghetto who had about 27,000 Jews.
The actual ghetto, in the sense that we understand ghetto, was in Bedzin established very late.
It was established at the beginning of 1943, just prior to the ethnic cleansing-- how I hate that expression-- just prior to the-- the Final Solution-- to the implementation of Final Solution.
We lived so far in the restricted areas.
We did not need guards because it was-- I mean, we were normal people.
We knew what it means to obey law.
You didn't have to teach people to-- to obey law.
That was ingrained.
Everybody knew that if it says don't, you don't.
So we lived in restricted areas.
We lived much-- in a much more condensed way than before.
But it was not appalling.
It was nothing like Warsaw or Litzmannstadt.
Nothing.
The ghetto was smaller.
The ghetto was productive.
The ghetto was open-- open to Poles who were willing to enter into a commercial transaction with Jews.
You sold what you had, you got supplement of food.
The smuggling was not that dangerous, because the ghetto was-- it was no ghetto.
It's not right to call that ghetto.
But it existed.
Yes, but not closed.
Not closed.
It wasn't even open ghetto, because we were scattered in all different parts of town.
It  were only certain streets on which we were allowed to live.
It was certain hours in which we were allowed to move.
And if we moved, we couldn't walk on footpaths.
We had to move in the middle of the road.
Absolutely.
Absolutely.
We didn't mingle with them, because within our areas-- for instance, if you wanted to go from point A to point B, you had to go to point from A to C to D to B.
You couldn't go the normal way in order to avoid, as much as you could, contact with Poles, and on certain-- at certain times.
The young people were employed.
Within these areas, where the Jews lived, was established everything was needed for life.
There were shops that supplied you with food, for which you had vouchers.
By the Germans to the Jewish [?

Judenrat. ?] The nominal role of the Judenrat-- that's the officers of the elders of Jews-- was to keep order within ghetto, and to attend to all sorts of Jewish affairs-- burials, sicknesses, hunger, a location where to live, distribution of food, distribution of clothing for the poor, and so on.
So actually, it was more, officially, more a social role.
And very soon it developed into an arm of Gestapo.
Well, by giving them an order, tomorrow you have to deliver me 5,000 Jews.
And this is where the moral corruption--
There was not a question of being prepared.
[DRINKS] There was not a question of being prepared.
Because by that time it became pretty obvious that the whole game was a game for survival.
And the misconception was that if you work for the Germans, as long as they need you, your chance of survival is bigger.
And this is where the horrible thing settled in, the disease of corruption-- the disease that I have to survive and no matter what price.
Because it was a disease of corruption.
You could bribe people to exempt you from certain tasks.
The Jews had to clear snow.
The Jews had to clear latrines.
You had a bit of money, you had a bit of connection, you went to the Jewish [GERMAN], to the Jewish council.
And you say, look, I pay for a poor man for two days' work.
Let him do the work.
There was nothing wrong with that sort of corruption, because by that time it was rather welcome.
But when you started to bribe the high officials, that's where the rot set in.
As long as you bought yourself out, and you provided work for someone else, there was, perhaps, a bit of a moral justification.

But when you had to provide people for something that was not defined-- the purpose was not defined; it was called for labor-- and when the people who came for the call up-- because at first it was all very nice.
You receive an advanced notice that tomorrow you have to present yourself at a certain--
Stop?
Ah, that tomorrow, at a certain time you have to present yourself at a certain place.
And you probably have heard the expression [GERMAN].
And when you saw who is coming to the places, you had no illusions that this is not for work.
They were the old, and the sick, the infirms, the abandoned children, the children from orphanage, mothers with children.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And although it was sane mind, there were no illusions to a mind of of any Jew.
The picture was not clear.
It was not clear.
Because the urge and the desire to leave was such that it obscured all logic--
[INAUDIBLE].
